title: Intro to multi-agent systems with ADK
summary: Google released the Agent Development Kit (ADK), a framework designed to simplify building and testing multi-agent systems. The tutorial demonstrates creating agents with custom tools including real-time date retrieval and Google Search integration, along with a built-in web interface for debugging.
writeup: Google's Agent Development Kit (ADK) provides a Python framework that handles the boilerplate of equipping LLMs with tools and running them in a loop, reducing a first agent to a handful of lines. The video walks from a bare "Hello World" agent through adding a datetime tool, then a built-in Google Search tool, and finally a multi-agent system where specialized agents are passed as tools to an orchestrator. The motivating example is a Top Chef search that surfaces hallucinated URLs, which a URL-verifier sub-agent corrects by fetching and reading each page, demonstrating the LLM-as-judge pattern. The through-line is that decomposing work across single-purpose agents with appropriately sized models gives finer control over accuracy, memory, and cost than a single agent loaded with many tools.
key_points: ADK ships two built-in testing interfaces: `adk run` launches a terminal dialogue and `adk web` spins up a local server at port 8000 with a visual stack trace panel, so debugging during development requires no external tooling.

Tools in ADK are ordinary Python functions appended to a `tools=[]` list on the agent definition; a `get_current_time` function returning `datetime.now()` is enough to give an agent real-time awareness the base LLM cannot provide.
The built-in Google Search tool requires `bypass_multiple_tools_limit=True` because a comprehensive search typically triggers several consecutive tool calls rather than a single one.

A multi-agent system is built by placing agent objects directly inside another agent's `tools=[]` list; the researcher orchestrator calls a search agent and then a URL-verifier agent in sequence, implementing LLM-as-judge without any custom orchestration code.

Assigning each sub-agent a single, narrow purpose allows different underlying models per agent: cheaper, faster models handle retrieval and verification subtasks while a more capable model drives the orchestrator reasoning step.
